romania.title: Prudența devine noul consum: românii amână creditele și planurile mari
romania.summary: Românii sunt mai atenți la modul în care își gestionează banii și aleg să prioritizeze cheltuielile esențiale. Aproape o treime (31%) dintre românii care nu vor să ia un credit în viitorul apropiat spun că preferă să fie mai precauți și să amâne orice fel de investiții care nu sunt urgente, precum renovarea locuinței, achiziția …

romania.title: Cinci setări prin care îți protejezi telefonul, în newsletter-ul Good Tech de pe 22 iulie
romania.summary: Înainte, dacă îți pierdeai telefonul, cea mai mare grijă era să nu pierzi lista de contacte și câteva fotografii. Astăzi, smartphone-ul a devenit cheia către aproape toată viața noastră digitală: aplicații bancare, parole salvate, documente, fotografii etc. În ediția de mâine, 22 iulie, a newsletter ului Good Tech, vei afla cinci pași simpli prin care …

romania.title: Cel mai puternic bancher de pe Wall Street afirmă că nu ar cumpăra acțiuni sau obligațiuni americane
romania.summary: Jamie Dimon, directorul general al băncii de investiții JPMorgan Chase, afirmă că investitorii subestimează riscurile cu care se confruntă economia globală și că, la prețurile actuale, el nu ar cumpăra nici acțiuni, nici obligațiuni cu scadență pe termen lung emise de Trezoreria Statelor Unite, relatează CNBC. Dimon a spus într-un amplu interviu acordat jurnalistului Wilfred …

Cum răspundem provocărilor generate de accelerarea ritmului de producție, consum și viață cotidiană? Pornind de… Articolul La aceeași masă pentru viitorul hranei. romania.title: 😂 Ca să reducă factura la curent, un român și-a trecut mașina de spălat pe GPL
romania.summary: Marian Coltea, un cetățean din județul Argeș a găsit o soluție inedită pentru reducerea facturii la curent electric, convertindu-și mașina de spălat rufe pentru a funcționa pe GPL. Bărbatul a instalat un sistem de alimentare cu gaz petrolier lichefiat, susținând că a reușit astfel să scadă semnificativ costurile lunare cu energia electrică. Potrivit declarațiilor sale, […] The post Ca să reducă factura la curent, un român și-a trecut mașina de spălat pe GPL appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: Mai sunt câteva ore și plicticosul campionat mondial de fotbal din America se încheie, lăsând loc adevăratului spectacol fotbalistic, Superliga românească la fotbal. Acum microbiștii vor putea vedea adevăratul fotbal dus pe viață și pe moarte, între echipe cu tradiție pe bătrânul continent, cum ar fi FCSB, Voluntari sau Csikalakacsikicha Miercurea Ciuc. Adio stadioanelor sterile […] The post Spectacolul revine la TV. Microbiștii vor vedea cum FCSB face 0-0 cu Cshikalakacshikicha M. Ciuc pe o tarla appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: Locație: Bacău | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

medical.title: How to keep babies safe when temperatures soar
medical.summary: Infants, toddlers and young children need outdoor activity to build strong bodies and minds, but they also require extra care in the heat.

medical.title: Last-line antibiotics at risk as children's resistance rises
medical.summary: Antimicrobial resistance in children is rising globally and will worsen over the next decade, threatening the effectiveness of life-saving antibiotics, according to the world's first monitoring platform. The study, led by Murdoch Children's Research Institute (MCRI) in collaboration with the University of Sydney, Clinton Health Access Initiative (CHAI) and The Chinese University of Hong Kong, analyzed more than 106,000 infection samples from children aged up to 18 years across 82 countries.

medical.title: Researchers analyzing colon cancer screenings find hope for higher retest rates
medical.summary: Nearly half of patients who completed an at-home test for colorectal cancer fail to repeat the screening consistently, according to Rutgers Health researchers, who also found primary care provider behaviors can increase patient consistency—and catch more cancers early.

medical.title: Researchers explain differences in brain tissue vulnerability in neurological disease
medical.summary: Many neurological diseases damage specific parts of the brain, even though the harmful protein involved is present throughout the brain. Researchers at Texas Children's Duncan Neurological Research Institute (Duncan NRI) and Baylor College of Medicine report new evidence that explains these differences in brain tissue vulnerability in a study published in Genes & Development.

medical.title: When it comes to brain health, is microbiome science missing much of the world?
medical.summary: For more than two decades, scientists have uncovered remarkable links between the trillions of microbes living in our gut and the health of our brains. This research has transformed our understanding of stress, depression, anxiety and neurodevelopment, opening new possibilities for preventing and treating mental illness. What if much of this science is based on studying only a narrow slice of global diversity?

medical.title: Largest genetic analysis of borderline personality disorder identifies 11 risk regions and nine genes
medical.summary: An international team led by the Central Institute of Mental Health in Mannheim has presented the largest genome-wide association study on borderline personality disorder to date. Data from about 13,000 affected people and more than 1.1 million controls from 14 countries were analyzed. The study identified 11 independent risk regions and nine genes that could contribute to the development of the disorder. The findings, published in Nature Genetics, show clear genetic overlaps with other mental and physical illnesses and open new perspectives for research and care.

medical.title: Researchers study barriers to GLP-1 insurance coverage
medical.summary: Despite growing evidence that GLP-1 medications improve health and are worth their cost, many insurance plans still limit coverage. University of Mississippi researchers are examining why and where that leaves patients.

medical.title: Keeping drugs free of contaminants—pharmaceutical manufacturers filter medications through tiny pores
medical.summary: Every year, the global pharmaceutical industry produces trillions of doses of medications. Manufacturing these drugs involves several stages, and the chance of contamination increases with every step.

medical.title: Green neighborhoods may help reduce long-term stress during pregnancy, new study finds
medical.summary: Pregnant women who live in greener neighborhoods may have lower levels of long-term stress, according to a new study led by researchers from Bar-Ilan University. The study found that greater exposure to residential greenery during pregnancy was associated with lower levels of cortisol and cortisone—two key stress hormones that can affect both maternal and fetal health.

science.title: 30,000 people tried for bigamy over the past 400 years in England and Wales, archives reveal
science.summary: Bigamy has been a rare crime during the past 400 years—and offenders have been harshly punished. It seems likely that no more than 30,000 individuals have ever been tried for bigamy in England and Wales during this period. Most have been convicted, and possible punishment has ranged from execution, branding, whipping, transportation and imprisonment to fines and community service in more recent decades.

science.title: Molecules stop carbon nanotubes clumping, unlocking record heat-to-electricity performance
science.summary: QUT researchers have overcome a challenge that has limited next-generation energy-harvesting materials for more than two decades, opening the door to more powerful wearable electronics and new ways of turning wasted heat into electricity. The breakthrough centers on carbon nanotubes, which are flexible, conductive microscopic rods that have long shown promise for wearable technologies but have been difficult to control.

science.title: Carbon nanotube model reveals how smart sensors perform at aircraft-like extremes
science.summary: Researchers from Skoltech, in collaboration with the Harbin Institute of Technology and Jiangsu University (China), have published a study presenting the first empirical model to explain and predict the sensing behavior of hierarchical tri-phase carbon nanotube systems across a wide temperature range (from -170°C (-274°F) to 90°C (194°F)). The study has been published in the Carbon journal.

science.title: Early career funding improves scientific influence and citations for women
science.summary: Early-career funding has a bigger impact on women than men when it comes to their careers in science, according to a new study from City St George's, University of London. While the study found that successful applicants in both groups secure more future research funding than unsuccessful applicants, it also found that successful female applicants receive more citations and are cited more often in their field than female applicants who did not receive funding.

science.title: Ancestral variation, not new mutations, may impact survival of species facing climate change
science.summary: Research co-authored by an Oklahoma Museum of Natural History curator, her graduate student and an international team of scientists has shed new light on the evolutionary "toolkit" animals use to survive environmental change.

science.title: NASA Sets Briefings for SpaceX Crew-13 Mission to Space Station
science.summary: NASA and its partners will discuss the upcoming crew rotation mission to the International Space Station during a pair of news conferences on Monday, Aug. 3, from the agency’s Johnson Space Center in Houston. Mission leadership will provide an overview of NASA’s SpaceX Crew‑13 mission at 12 p.m. EDT. Next, crew members will discuss their […]

science.title: NASA, GE Aerospace Work Enables Hybrid-Electric Flight Demonstration
science.summary: An aircraft powered by a megawatt-class hybrid-electric engine developed in collaboration with NASA and built by GE Aerospace, demonstrated flight of an innovation that can inform new generations of fuel-saving aircraft power systems. Mounted to a Saab 340B aircraft, the engine flew at Farnborough International Air Show in the United Kingdom. It was the public […]

science.title: New NASA Earth Missions Gear Up to Start Science Flights
science.summary: The first of six new airborne campaigns will hit the skies this summer.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: ANIS lansează un asistent AI care explică pe înțelesul tuturor cum poate inovația să adauge până la 16 miliarde de euro anual economiei României
mix_items.summary: ANIS a lansat ANIS.Innovation.AI, un asistent conversațional bazat pe inteligență artificială care transformă în răspunsuri ușor de înțeles concluziile celor mai recente studii despre inovația din...

mix_items.title: Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Descoperă povestea Alinei și cum sprijinul oferit de Fundația Inocenți a ajutat-o să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile școlare și curajul de a reuși. The post Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Evaluarea Națională 2026: Tranziția către liceu, punctul critic al abandonului școlar. World Vision România propune un plan de intervenție
mix_items.summary: București, 1 iulie. Rezultatele Evaluării Naționale 2026 reconfirmă decalajele profunde dintre copiii din mediul rural și cei din mediul urban... Articolul Evaluarea Națională 2026: Tranziția către liceu, punctul critic al abandonului școlar. World Vision România propune un plan de intervenție apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: 30,000 people tried for bigamy over the past 400 years in England and Wales, archives reveal
mix_items.summary: Bigamy has been a rare crime during the past 400 years—and offenders have been harshly punished. It seems likely that no more than 30,000 individuals have ever been tried for bigamy in England and Wales during this period. Most have been convicted, and possible punishment has ranged from execution, branding, whipping, transportation and imprisonment to fines and community service in more recent decades.

mix_items.title: Părinte Social
mix_items.summary: Părinte Social (Mamă SOS) – București și Cisnădie, Sibiu Transformă un loc de muncă într-o misiune de viață La SOS Satele Copiilor România, credem că fiecare copil are dreptul să... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: Bacău | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Molecules stop carbon nanotubes clumping, unlocking record heat-to-electricity performance
mix_items.summary: QUT researchers have overcome a challenge that has limited next-generation energy-harvesting materials for more than two decades, opening the door to more powerful wearable electronics and new ways of turning wasted heat into electricity. The breakthrough centers on carbon nanotubes, which are flexible, conductive microscopic rods that have long shown promise for wearable technologies but have been difficult to control.

mix_items.title: Carbon nanotube model reveals how smart sensors perform at aircraft-like extremes
mix_items.summary: Researchers from Skoltech, in collaboration with the Harbin Institute of Technology and Jiangsu University (China), have published a study presenting the first empirical model to explain and predict the sensing behavior of hierarchical tri-phase carbon nanotube systems across a wide temperature range (from -170°C (-274°F) to 90°C (194°F)). The study has been published in the Carbon journal.

mix_items.title: Early career funding improves scientific influence and citations for women
mix_items.summary: Early-career funding has a bigger impact on women than men when it comes to their careers in science, according to a new study from City St George's, University of London. While the study found that successful applicants in both groups secure more future research funding than unsuccessful applicants, it also found that successful female applicants receive more citations and are cited more often in their field than female applicants who did not receive funding.

mix_items.title: Ancestral variation, not new mutations, may impact survival of species facing climate change
mix_items.summary: Research co-authored by an Oklahoma Museum of Natural History curator, her graduate student and an international team of scientists has shed new light on the evolutionary "toolkit" animals use to survive environmental change.

mix_items.title: NASA Sets Briefings for SpaceX Crew-13 Mission to Space Station
mix_items.summary: NASA and its partners will discuss the upcoming crew rotation mission to the International Space Station during a pair of news conferences on Monday, Aug. 3, from the agency’s Johnson Space Center in Houston. Mission leadership will provide an overview of NASA’s SpaceX Crew‑13 mission at 12 p.m. EDT. Next, crew members will discuss their […]

mix_items.title: How to keep babies safe when temperatures soar
mix_items.summary: Infants, toddlers and young children need outdoor activity to build strong bodies and minds, but they also require extra care in the heat.

mix_items.title: Last-line antibiotics at risk as children's resistance rises
mix_items.summary: Antimicrobial resistance in children is rising globally and will worsen over the next decade, threatening the effectiveness of life-saving antibiotics, according to the world's first monitoring platform. The study, led by Murdoch Children's Research Institute (MCRI) in collaboration with the University of Sydney, Clinton Health Access Initiative (CHAI) and The Chinese University of Hong Kong, analyzed more than 106,000 infection samples from children aged up to 18 years across 82 countries.

mix_items.title: Researchers analyzing colon cancer screenings find hope for higher retest rates
mix_items.summary: Nearly half of patients who completed an at-home test for colorectal cancer fail to repeat the screening consistently, according to Rutgers Health researchers, who also found primary care provider behaviors can increase patient consistency—and catch more cancers early.

mix_items.title: NASA, GE Aerospace Work Enables Hybrid-Electric Flight Demonstration
mix_items.summary: An aircraft powered by a megawatt-class hybrid-electric engine developed in collaboration with NASA and built by GE Aerospace, demonstrated flight of an innovation that can inform new generations of fuel-saving aircraft power systems. Mounted to a Saab 340B aircraft, the engine flew at Farnborough International Air Show in the United Kingdom. It was the public […]

mix_items.title: Researchers explain differences in brain tissue vulnerability in neurological disease
mix_items.summary: Many neurological diseases damage specific parts of the brain, even though the harmful protein involved is present throughout the brain. Researchers at Texas Children's Duncan Neurological Research Institute (Duncan NRI) and Baylor College of Medicine report new evidence that explains these differences in brain tissue vulnerability in a study published in Genes & Development.

mix_items.title: New NASA Earth Missions Gear Up to Start Science Flights
mix_items.summary: The first of six new airborne campaigns will hit the skies this summer.

mix_items.title: When it comes to brain health, is microbiome science missing much of the world?
mix_items.summary: For more than two decades, scientists have uncovered remarkable links between the trillions of microbes living in our gut and the health of our brains. This research has transformed our understanding of stress, depression, anxiety and neurodevelopment, opening new possibilities for preventing and treating mental illness. What if much of this science is based on studying only a narrow slice of global diversity?

mix_items.title: Largest genetic analysis of borderline personality disorder identifies 11 risk regions and nine genes
mix_items.summary: An international team led by the Central Institute of Mental Health in Mannheim has presented the largest genome-wide association study on borderline personality disorder to date. Data from about 13,000 affected people and more than 1.1 million controls from 14 countries were analyzed. The study identified 11 independent risk regions and nine genes that could contribute to the development of the disorder. The findings, published in Nature Genetics, show clear genetic overlaps with other mental and physical illnesses and open new perspectives for research and care.

mix_items.title: Researchers study barriers to GLP-1 insurance coverage
mix_items.summary: Despite growing evidence that GLP-1 medications improve health and are worth their cost, many insurance plans still limit coverage. University of Mississippi researchers are examining why and where that leaves patients.

mix_items.title: Keeping drugs free of contaminants—pharmaceutical manufacturers filter medications through tiny pores
mix_items.summary: Every year, the global pharmaceutical industry produces trillions of doses of medications. Manufacturing these drugs involves several stages, and the chance of contamination increases with every step.

mix_items.title: Green neighborhoods may help reduce long-term stress during pregnancy, new study finds
mix_items.summary: Pregnant women who live in greener neighborhoods may have lower levels of long-term stress, according to a new study led by researchers from Bar-Ilan University. The study found that greater exposure to residential greenery during pregnancy was associated with lower levels of cortisol and cortisone—two key stress hormones that can affect both maternal and fetal health.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Mi-am verificat contul si el mi-a verificat caracterul.

satire_ro.title: Sportul renaşte! Naţionala de cocalari a României s-a calificat la Insula Mondială a Iubirii din 2028
